articles.headline: Destruction of the Alton Observer press and the mobbing of Elijah Lovejoy
articles.summary: Reprints a letter from a gentleman's brother in Alton, Illinois describing the night mob attack (c. 10pm) that destroyed Elijah P. Lovejoy's Alton Observer press: 20-30 men stoned the office, an armed party concealed on a rooftop across the street covered the room, the mob forced the back door, threw the press and materials into the street and river, and the ruins littered a quarter mile in every direction; roughly 400 citizens watched, most cheering. Frames as the 'only fearless champion of the rights of man in this state' facing repeated mob violence; notes Lovejoy has been threatened and once stopped in the street. Accompanies continuing coverage of the Cornish-Whipper-Hinton exchange over the American Moral Reform Society/Moral Reform Convention's direction on the term 'colored,' and 'Municipal Oppression' editorial below.

articles.headline: Municipal Oppression -- denial of carman/porter licenses to Black New Yorkers
articles.summary: Editorial denouncing the New York City Corporation's practice of licensing carmen and porters exclusively to white applicants regardless of character, while denying colored citizens -- among them some of the city's 'very best citizens' -- the same trade licenses under the pretext that licensing them would provoke violent collision with white carmen. Cites a specific case: a colored citizen's respectful license petition, endorsed by a gentleman who himself later became Mayor of New York, was denied by that same man once in office.

articles.headline: 'A Boy Kidnapped' -- David Ruggles exposes attempted sale of apprentice Edward Watson
articles.author: David Ruggles
articles.summary: David Ruggles reports that a West Indian named Ayres imported an apprentice from Jamaica, Edward Watson, and shipped him aboard the brig Buenos-Ayres (Capt. Stewart) bound for South Carolina, intending to sell him for cash for want of friends to defend him. After the facts were confirmed, an application for a writ of habeas corpus was refused by Judge Irving (citing illness) and then by Judge Ulshoerfer (who 'wanted his dinner'); by the time relief could be obtained the vessel had sailed and Ayres had absconded south by land with the boy.

articles.headline: Dr. James McCune Smith's Anti-Phrenology lecture notice; Juvenile Daughters of Ruth anniversary; Philadelphia craftsmen's notices
articles.summary: James McCune Smith, M.D., announces a lecture refuting Phrenology at Philomathean Hall, Duane near Hudson St., Sept. 18. Separately, the Juvenile Daughters of Ruth announce their anniversary celebration (Sept. 20, Zion's Church, speaking and singing by young ladies of the society), signed Ann Matilda Harrington, President. Also: Francis Wiles announces his boarding house's removal to 119 West Broadway; William J. Wilson, boot maker, removed to 1 Ann-street; Phoenix High School (Miss Merritt, instructress) reopening notice signed by Theodore S. Wright, President.

articles.headline: Prominent abolitionists' endorsement of the Colored American
articles.author: Arthur Tappan, Joshua Leavitt, Lewis Tappan, Sim. S. Jocelyn, R. G. Williams, E. Wright Jr., Gerrit Smith, Peter Williams, A. A. Phelps, Theo. S. Wright
articles.summary: A signed endorsement letter from ten prominent (mostly white) abolitionist and reform figures recommending the Colored American to the patronage of Abolitionists nationwide and especially to colored fellow-citizens, expressing confidence in editor Samuel E. Cornish's abilities and discretion. Also on this page: Alexander Dubois of New Haven advertises a house and lot for sale on Washington Street near Bishop's Hotel (Aug. 23, 1837) -- likely the New Haven grandfather of W. E. B. Du Bois; flagged for future research, no counterpart DB record yet to merge against.

articles.headline: J. C. Beman's report on the colored community of Providence, RI
articles.author: J. C. Beman
articles.summary: Beman reports Providence's colored population at about 400, worshipping in one church (53 members) under the pastoral care of Rev. J. W. Lewis, in connection with the white Baptists; describes an internal church dispute over a breakaway congregation. Lists three schools for colored children (one taught by Mr. Ransom Parker, 60 scholars), a Temperance Society with 170 members, and 25 Black property-owning taxpayers in the city.

articles.headline: 'Annual Convention of the People of Color' -- history and revival appeal, by 'Hamilton' (part 1)
articles.author: Hamilton
articles.summary: First installment of a historical retrospective on the national Colored Conventions (1830-1835), prompted by the American Moral Reform Society's recent convention. Names past Convention delegates as men 'whose hearts did not err': Hinton, Bowers, Whipper, Morel, Hamilton, Sipkins, Jennings, Bell, Vogelsang, Hughes, Shadd, Vashon, Cowley, and others. Recalls the opening address of an unnamed venerable early Convention President. Calls for the annual Conventions' revival and appeals to 'Whipper, Hinton, Bowers, Forten, Purvis, Butler, Cassey and others' of Philadelphia to help lead the effort. Author promises a fuller Convention history in future numbers.

articles.headline: William Yates's continuing research on New York's legal disabilities of colored citizens
articles.author: William Yates
articles.summary: Writing from Albany (Sept. 8, 1837), attorney William Yates describes researching the legal disabilities of free people of color in New York State using the State Library's statute collections, in preparation for a book, and sends Cornish a detailed table of contents outlining eight categories of disability (exclusion from representation, unequal voting rights, naturalization/alien land-holding bars, militia service exemption/disqualification, unequal taxation, denial of jury trial in some cases, the 1817/1841 gradual-emancipation statutes' effects, and colonial-era slave-trade prohibitions with exceptions). Also on this page: Rev. Stephen Gloucester (elder of a Philadelphia colored Presbyterian church, son of the late Rev. John Gloucester) announces a fundraising Fair at the Broadway Tabernacle for his church's building debt, assisted by Philadelphia ladies.

articles.headline: 'Publicola' letter to Mayor Aaron Clark on New York grog shops
articles.author: Publicola (pseudonym)
articles.summary: An open letter to New York Mayor Aaron Clark urging him to use his office to suppress the licensing of grog shops, blamed for widows, orphans, and moral/physical ruin citywide; invokes Dr. Benjamin Rush's dictum that alcohol has destroyed more than sword and pestilence combined, and warns Clark against the 'trucking, vacillating policy' that the writer says has permanently disgraced Martin Van Buren's name.

articles.headline: Commercial Advertiser reprint: Dr. James McCune Smith's Phrenology lecture
articles.summary: A friendly New York Commercial Advertiser review, reprinted, of Dr. James McCune Smith's lecture 'on the fallacy of phrenology' at Philomathean Hall -- praises his modest demeanor and rigorous argument, and gives biographical detail: returned from Glasgow after several years' study, holding a Master of Arts and having graduated in medicine at the University of Glasgow, now settling in New York as a physician. Same page: 'Interesting Examination' reports the Colored Female School of Newark's public exam (reading, writing, grammar, arithmetic, geography) under instructress Miss Sawyer, attended and praised by Newark's Mayor, the Hon. Mr. Frelinghuysen, who spoke on colored education's importance.

articles.headline: 'Prejudice in the Church' -- colored ministers barred from white pulpits
articles.summary: First installment of a series arguing prejudice against color is the church's most unreasonable, wicked, and fatal sin; the unnamed author (a colored minister, seventeen years in the ministry) describes being personally excluded for sixteen years from any invitation to preach in white brethren's pulpits, or to be appointed to any ecclesiastical duty, alongside 'brother Wright' (Theodore S. Wright), despite good standing; states white congregations would rather close their churches than admit a colored minister to preach.

articles.headline: Rev. Daniel A. Payne declines invitation to address the NY State Anti-Slavery Society
articles.author: Daniel A. Payne
articles.summary: Writing from Troy, NY (Sept. 18, 1837), initially signed only 'A Colored Clergyman of Troy, New York' but attributed at the letter's close to Daniel A. Payne, this deeply emotional letter declines the New York State Anti-Slavery Society Executive Committee's invitation to address its annual meeting, citing being overcome by grief at slavery's horrors (recalling seeing enslaved mothers and children separated, and people driven in chains to the New Orleans market) rather than any lack of will; closes invoking 'holy freedom.'

articles.headline: Joseph Gaving's letter to David Ruggles: three native Africans fraudulently shipped south
articles.author: Joseph Gaving
articles.summary: Writing from New Orleans (Jan. 6, 1837, but printed here referencing the NY Vigilance Committee's first annual report), Joseph Gaving reports that a colored man and seamen's landlord, John Russell, fraudulently and forcibly shipped three native Africans -- named Joan (or John), Robin, and Joan -- aboard a New Orleans-bound vessel under Capt. J. Shephard without money or clothing; the captain repeatedly tried to sell them and mistreated them 'like dogs.' Gaving, who tried to intervene, urges David Ruggles's Vigilance Committee to investigate and publicly expose Russell.

articles.headline: 'Appeal to Presbyterians' -- a minister's plea over his children's school exclusion
articles.author: 'Thy Doulos' (pseudonym)
articles.summary: An anonymous Presbyterian minister of color (17 years in the ministry, 24 years a professed Christian) appeals to the Presbyterians of New York City: after 14 years of itinerant ministry he resettled in the city four years ago for his children's education, but every Presbyterian school and seminary he applied to denied admission to his four children (ages 13, 11, 10, 3, mixed blood) solely on account of complexion. Appeals for the churches, many linked to seminaries, to open admission, pledging to cover tuition costs.

articles.headline: History of the Wilberforce Colony Society and the Conventions of the People of Color, by 'Hamilton' (part 2)
articles.author: Hamilton
articles.summary: Continuing from the Sept. 23 installment: traces the 1830 origin of the national Colored Conventions to Ohio's oppressive 1829 'Black Laws,' which drove refugees to seek asylum elsewhere, prompting a New York public meeting (July 1830, Abyssinian Baptist Church) that formed the Wilberforce Colony Society to investigate a colony in Upper Canada. Names its committee of conference: Peter Williams, Thomas L. Jennings, Peter Vogelsang, Theodore S. Wright, and Benjamin [surname unclear in OCR]. Notes correspondence with Black leaders in Boston, Philadelphia, and Baltimore produced one reply, from Mr. J. E. Gates of Baltimore, proposing a national Convention of the People of Color -- the origin of the annual Conventions.

articles.headline: 'Slavery. Scene in Philadelphia' -- fugitive-slave trial of a woman of white appearance
articles.summary: An anonymous eyewitness describes a fugitive-slave hearing at the Chestnut Street courthouse in Philadelphia on Aug. 1 (year not given in this excerpt): a crowded courtroom awaiting the judge's ruling on a woman 'claimed as property' whose appearance was that of a white lady, married about five years with one entirely white-appearing infant son about two years old. The account breaks off as the judge is about to speak; names of the woman, her husband, and the judge are not given in the surviving text -- likely continued in a subsequent (not yet OCR'd) issue.

articles.headline: 'Sketch of Churches and Ecclesiastical Organizations among the People of Color' -- Richard Allen, Absalom Jones, and the founding of Bethel AME
articles.summary: A detailed history (furnished by an Anti-Slavery Society agent) of Black Methodism's separate organization, recounting the 1793 St. George's Church (Philadelphia) incident -- on Rev. Richard Allen's own authority -- in which a white church officer forcibly interrupted Rev. Absalom Jones ('Rev. A. Jones,' OCR'd 'A. J----- Jones') mid-prayer to remove him from the main floor to the gallery, prompting the colored congregants to walk out en masse and found Bethel Church. Reproduces original 1794 Bethel Church minutes naming founding members John Allen, Richard Allen, Thomas Martin, Solomon Brittenham, and Prince Pruence, and a trustees'/committee list including John Morris, Philip Johnston, William Hogan, Richard Allen, Anthony Roberts, and John Allen; notes Dr. Benjamin Rush's early support and his customary pew in the church. Includes 1837 African Methodist Episcopal Church membership tables by conference/circuit (Philadelphia, New York, Ohio, Baltimore Conferences) totaling several thousand members, and identifies Rt. Rev. Morris Brown of Philadelphia as Bishop and Rt. Rev. Edward Waters of Baltimore as Assistant Bishop.

articles.headline: Augustus Wattles's report on colored schools in Ohio
articles.author: Augustus Wattles
articles.summary: Wattles reports on colored schools across numerous Ohio settlements (Zanesfield/Logan Co., Big Bottom/Pike Co., Lancaster, Urbana, Rush Co. Indiana, and settlements on Brush Creek and Straight Creek in Brown Co., plus Massillon and Cleveland), giving pupil counts (600-700 total across listed schools) and noting 14 of the teachers are women, mostly abolitionists, some qualified for the best schools in the country. Calls for more teachers in several named locations needing schools.

articles.headline: 'A Caution' -- fraud warning against 'Dr.' Israel Williams
articles.author: unsigned (Philadelphia correspondent, departing for Europe)
articles.summary: A New York correspondent about to depart for Europe publicly warns against 'Israel Williams,' alias 'Dr. Williams of Lancaster, Pa.,' who on Sept. 22, 1837 broke into the writer's trunk and stole about $70 in specie (plus an earlier $10 bill lifted from his pocket), confirmed found on him by a police officer; Williams remains at large for lack of prosecution given the writer's imminent departure.

articles.headline: C. B. Ray's travel letter from Geneva, NY
articles.author: C. B. Ray
articles.summary: General Agent Charles B. Ray reports Geneva's colored population at about 250, mostly formerly enslaved Virginians brought north and later emancipated by their masters, generally comfortable, many owning their own homes; describes visiting an elderly formerly-enslaved farmer near the town. Notes Geneva's white residents are largely unsympathetic to abolition. Forwards subscriber names and payments collected; continuing on to Rochester next.

events.name: Public meeting welcoming Dr. James McCune Smith's return from Glasgow -- New York
events.location: Lecture Room of the Broadway Tabernacle, New York City
events.description: Notice of a public meeting of friends and admirers of Dr. James McCune Smith, A.M., to be held Tuesday evening, Sept. 19, 1837, expressing cordial welcome and high esteem for his professional and literary character on his return from five years' medical study at the University of Glasgow.

events.name: Third Anniversary of the Ladies' Literary Society of the City of New-York
events.description: Anniversary celebration with an order of exercises (readings, original compositions, music, dialogues on temperance and conduct) performed by young women of the Society; the address 'On the Improvement of the Mind' invokes Abigail A. Matthews as an exhorting voice to the Society's young women.
attendees: address on improvement of the mind / poetic address

events.name: Public meeting on the Right of Suffrage -- Philomathean Hall, New York
events.description: Public meeting of the Colored Young Men called for Monday evening, Sept. 25, 1837 to advance suffrage petitions to the state legislature; petitions were to be available for signing. Called by order of the Young Men's General Committee.

events.name: Mental and Moral Improvement Society's public reception for Dr. James McCune Smith -- Troy, NY
events.location: Lecture room of the Liberty Street Presbyterian Church, Troy, NY
events.description: Public meeting of the Mental and Moral Improvement Society of Troy, together with respectable colored inhabitants, to express unqualified esteem and welcome for Dr. James McCune Smith on his return from the University of Glasgow. A complimentary address praising his 'blooming, deathless laurels' won abroad was read and unanimously adopted on motion of William Rich, seconded by Robert Churchall.
attendees: Vice President; moved adoption of address

agents.note: New general-agent appointments (by C. B. Ray), Oct. 1837
